text: Old mode: a human writes every single prompt by hand.
New mode: the human designs a loop architecture that prompts agents repeatedly.
A loop is a recursive goal with cadence, verification, and memory.
Execution cycles through iterate, verify, and handoff when needed.
The core shift is this: design the system that prompts your agents.
Voices across the ecosystem now say loops are replacing ad-hoc prompting.
This operating model is built for Grok, Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor workflows.
The leverage point shifted from prompt craft to control-system design over time.
With urgency established, we can now break down the architecture building blocks.
Scheduling sets loop cadence for discovery and triage.
Worktrees provide safe parallel execution for concurrent changes.
Skills preserve reusable project knowledge across sessions.
Connectors and plugins extend loops into real tools and systems.
Sub-agents split maker and checker responsibilities.
Memory and state are the durable backbone across every run.
The pattern catalog turns loop engineering into reusable execution recipes.
Cadence differs: some loops run daily, others every few minutes.
Risk and cost profiles vary sharply across pattern types.
Pattern picker helps teams choose by constraints, not hype.
Rollout is phased from report-only toward higher autonomy.
Pattern choice is a governance decision with explicit trade-offs.
Loop-init scaffolds a starter loop from a chosen pattern and tool.
Loop-cost estimates expected token spend before rollout.
Loop-audit scores readiness and flags missing controls.
Before-and-after demo shows maturity from empty state to L1 and L2.
Operational governance becomes measurable, reviewable engineering work.
Failure modes and anti-patterns define what breaks first in unattended loops.
Multiple loops can collide without coordination and shared state discipline.
Security posture must constrain connector scope and unattended execution risk.
Human-gate branches decide whether to execute directly or escalate with context.
Automate execution aggressively, but never automate accountability.
Start narrow with one loop and one bounded objective.
Track outcomes, incidents, and operating cost over time.
Progress from L1 report-only to L2 assisted fixes and then selective L3.
Loop engineering delivers long-term durability through iterative systems design.
